description: What digital forensics interviews test: artifact knowledge (Windows, Linux, macOS), evidence handling and chain of custody, tool proficiency, timeline reconstruction, and ability to prove or disprove specific attacker actions from physical artifacts. Tests precision - forensics is about what you can PROVE, not what you suspect.

junior.label: Junior / Associate
characteristics: Knows forensics tools by name (Autopsy, FTK, Volatility) but cannot describe a workflow without prompting
Understands evidence imaging at a conceptual level but cannot explain why write blockers are used
Cannot explain the difference between a forensic image (bit-for-bit) and a logical copy
Knows that memory forensics exists but cannot name a single volatility plugin or what it reveals
Thinks 'deleted file' recovery means running Recuva on the live system
Cannot distinguish between artifact types: system artifacts vs. user artifacts vs. application artifacts
Has heard of the Windows Registry as a forensic artifact but cannot name specific keys relevant to investigation
Cannot explain what prefetch files are or what investigation questions they answer
Describes forensic process as 'image the drive and look for evidence'
Does not understand timestamp types (MAC times: Modified, Accessed, Created) or their forensic significance
Cannot explain chain of custody or why it matters
Has no understanding of volatile data and the order of volatility
Does not know what a forensic hash is used for in evidence integrity verification
Cannot identify suspicious artifacts in a simple case scenario
Thinks all forensics is Windows-only
example_phrases: I'd use Autopsy to analyze the disk image
You image the drive first so you don't destroy evidence
I know Volatility is for memory forensics
I'd look through the files on the disk for anything suspicious
I'd check the recently accessed files to see what the user was doing
We'd hash the evidence to preserve its integrity
I'm familiar with the Windows Registry but I'd need to look up the specific keys
red_flags: Claims forensics experience but cannot explain write blocker purpose
Says 'I'd analyze the evidence' without explaining what artifacts they'd prioritize
Confuses forensic imaging with backup copy
Cannot name a single forensic artifact beyond 'event logs'
Thinks chain of custody is only relevant in legal cases, not internal investigations
Has never actually run a Volatility plugin against a memory dump
Does not know what NTFS means or that it's a filesystem relevant to forensics

mid.label: Mid-Level
characteristics: Knows key Windows forensic artifacts and their locations: Prefetch (C:\Windows\Prefetch), LNK files (C:\Users\<user>\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Windows\Recent), Shellbags (NTUSER.DAT), Jump Lists, SRUM database
Can explain what each artifact proves: Prefetch proves execution, LNK proves file access, Shellbags prove folder browsing
Understands NTFS timestamps (MACE: Modified, Accessed, Created, Entry Modified) and can explain timestomping
Can run basic Volatility 3 plugins: windows.pslist, windows.cmdline, windows.netscan, windows.malfind
Understands Windows Registry hives and investigatively relevant keys: SYSTEM, SOFTWARE, NTUSER.DAT, SAM
Knows common registry forensic locations: UserAssist (run programs), MUICache, RecentDocs, BAM/DAM (background activity)
Can perform a basic memory analysis: identify injected code with malfind, find network connections with netscan
Understands what $MFT (Master File Table) contains and can use it to recover deleted file metadata
Knows the $J (USN Journal) and $LogFile for NTFS transaction tracking
Can perform timeline analysis using multiple artifact sources and create a coherent narrative
Understands hash verification for evidence integrity and can explain what a hash mismatch means
Has analyzed phishing or malware cases end-to-end: email headers, extracted payload, sandbox analysis, persistence identification
Knows Linux forensic artifacts: /var/log, bash_history, cron jobs, /etc/passwd and /etc/shadow, auth.log, systemd logs
Can acquire a forensic image using dd, dcfldd, or FTK Imager and verify with MD5/SHA-256
Understands the difference between live response (triage) and dead-box forensics and when to use each
example_phrases: For execution evidence I'd check Prefetch first - each .pf file contains the executable name, run count, and last run time for up to 128 entries
The LNK file in the user's Recent folder had a created timestamp before the file existed on the suspect machine, which proved the file came from an external source
I ran volatility's malfind plugin and found a process with executable pages that had no corresponding file on disk - that's a strong indicator of process injection
The $MFT entry showed the file was created but the NTFS timestamps were from 1970, which is a classic timestomping indicator
I pulled UserAssist from NTUSER.DAT with RegRipper - the ROT-13 encoded entries showed the attacker ran three specific tools that weren't in the Prefetch directory because they'd been deleted

Chain of custody started with imaging - I hashed the original drive before and after imaging, documented the hardware write blocker serial number, and stored the image with the original hash in the evidence locker
red_flags: Cannot name the location of Prefetch files or what they prove
Does not know what Shellbags are or what investigative question they answer
Cannot explain what MACE timestamps are in the NTFS context
Thinks memory forensics requires a system crash (blue screen)
Cannot explain what $MFT is without looking it up
Has never actually performed timeline correlation from multiple artifact sources
Claims to know Volatility but cannot name a single plugin and what it outputs
expected_tools: Eric Zimmermann tools suite (PECmd, LECmd, JLECmd, AppCompatCacheParser)
expected_frameworks: NIST SP 800-86 (Guide to Integrating Forensic Techniques)
RFC 3227 (Guidelines for Evidence Collection and Archiving)
Order of volatility in live response
ACPO Good Practice Guide for Digital Evidence (UK context)

senior.label: Senior
characteristics: Can reconstruct a complete attacker timeline from raw artifacts with specific timestamps down to the second
Identifies anti-forensics techniques and works around them: timestomping, log clearing (EID 1102), prefetch disabling, track covering
Has performed memory forensics on live systems and knows the risks of volatility change during acquisition
Knows cloud forensic considerations: AWS S3 access logs, Azure Blob audit, container forensics, ephemeral instances
Can perform macOS forensics: APFS artifacts, Unified Log (ULS), FSEvents, plist files, spotlight metadata
Can analyze Linux malware artifacts: crontab persistence, LD_PRELOAD hijacks, /tmp execution, kernel module rootkits
Has experience with mobile forensics concepts: iOS ATC databases, Android logcat, app sandbox artifacts
Understands Encase/FTK's evidence file formats (E01, AFF4) and can work with them
Can identify browser artifacts at investigation depth: Chrome/Firefox SQLite databases, cache, extensions
Has testified or prepared reports suitable for legal proceedings
Can perform network forensics: pcap analysis, Zeek logs, reconstructing file transfers from network capture
Understands Windows volume shadow copies (VSS) and their forensic value for historical artifact recovery
Has experience with enterprise-scale forensics: Velociraptor, GRR, CrowdStrike for fleet-wide collection
Can identify indicators of a wiped or overwritten disk and recover evidence despite anti-forensics
Understands the forensic implications of full-disk encryption (BitLocker keys, memory-based key recovery)
example_phrases: I found the attacker had cleared the Security event log (EID 1102), but they forgot about the PowerShell operational logs and the Sysmon logs which gave me the same timeline
The container had been stopped and replaced, but the orchestration platform retained the previous container logs for 24 hours - I grabbed those immediately before they rotated out
The macOS Unified Log had the launch daemon activation event at 3:47 AM, which correlated with the external network connection in the Zeek logs 12 seconds later

VSS had three snapshots from before the ransomware deployed. I recovered the attacker's staging directory from the T-72h snapshot, which contained the exfiltration tool with its original creation timestamp
BitLocker was enabled but the key was in memory - I took a live memory image first, extracted the FVEK using Volatility's bitlocker plugin, then decrypted the disk offline
For the legal proceeding I documented every tool, version, hash, and command in the examination log so the methodology could be independently reproduced
red_flags: Has never performed forensics on a macOS or Linux system
Cannot explain how to recover forensic value from a log-cleared system
Does not know what Volume Shadow Copies are used for in forensics
Has never prepared a forensic report for legal use
Cannot explain what Velociraptor is used for in enterprise forensics
Has no experience with cloud or container forensics
expected_tools: Velociraptor (VQL hunting and forensic collection at scale)
Eric Zimmermann full suite (PECmd, LECmd, AppCompatCacheParser, RBCmd, WxTCmd)
macOS: macOS Unified Log viewer, macOS Artifact Parsing Tool (mac_apt)
expected_frameworks: Scientific method in forensics (hypothesis, evidence, conclusion)
Legal hold and evidence handling for litigation support

staff.label: Staff / Principal
characteristics: Designs the DFIR program architecture: tooling stack, evidence retention policy, legal hold procedures, collection workflows
Has expert witness experience or has prepared forensic reports that withstood cross-examination
Develops custom forensic tools or Velociraptor artifacts for novel collection challenges
Has worked cases involving nation-state actors and understands the operational security implications of the investigation itself
Can lead forensic investigations across multiple jurisdictions with data sovereignty considerations
Has designed forensic labs and evidence storage with chain of custody controls
Contributes to the DFIR community: public tools, blog posts, conference research (e.g., OSDFCon, SANS DFIR Summit)
Understands regulatory requirements that govern forensic investigations in their sector
Can evaluate and select forensic platforms for enterprise deployment
Has designed large-scale forensic collection operations across hundreds or thousands of endpoints
Understands the intersection of forensics with legal discovery (eDiscovery) and can manage that relationship
Has investigated insider threat cases from start to legal referral
Can advise engineering teams on how to build systems that are forensically sound (logging, audit trails)
Has experience with dark web investigations and open-source intelligence (OSINT) integration into forensics
example_phrases: We built a custom Velociraptor artifact to collect the proprietary application logs that no off-the-shelf tool understood - that's now part of our standard collection profile

The 14 exabytes of data we collected had to go through legal review in three different jurisdictions before we could analyze it. I designed the data segregation workflow so the legal teams could control access by geography
I testified for six hours about the forensic methodology. The opposing counsel tried to attack chain of custody, but because every step had hash verification and timestamped documentation, we held up

After the nation-state intrusion, we had to assume the adversary was watching our investigation. We conducted the entire operation out-of-band with dedicated collection infrastructure they hadn't touched
I built the enterprise evidence retention policy based on litigation hold requirements, regulatory minimums, and storage cost optimization. It saves $400k annually and reduces legal risk
red_flags: Has never testified or prepared a forensic report for legal use
Cannot explain data sovereignty implications for multi-jurisdiction investigations
Has no experience designing a forensic program from scratch
Cannot explain what eDiscovery is or how forensics intersects with it
Has never contributed anything to the DFIR community
expected_tools: Enterprise forensic platforms (Magnet Axiom, Nuix, Relativity for eDiscovery)
Custom Python/Go tooling for novel collection
Legal hold and eDiscovery platforms (Reveal, Exterro)
expected_frameworks: Daubert Standard / Frye Standard (US forensic evidence admissibility)
ISO 27037 (Guidelines for evidence identification, collection, acquisition and preservation)
SANS FOR508, FOR518, FOR572, FOR610 (deep expertise in relevant courses)

positive: Speaks in terms of what an artifact PROVES, not just what it suggests
Immediately considers evidence preservation before analysis
Knows specific file paths and registry keys, not just artifact names
Identifies anti-forensic techniques unprompted when discussing artifacts
Ties forensic findings back to specific attacker actions (execution, lateral movement, exfiltration)
Considers what the absence of an artifact means (intentional deletion vs. artifact not applicable)
Can explain the forensic significance of timestamps: which are reliable and which can be manipulated
Mentions chain of custody and integrity verification without prompting
negative: Describes forensics as 'looking through files for suspicious stuff'
Cannot name a specific artifact and what it proves
Has never used Volatility against an actual memory dump
Thinks forensics only applies to criminal cases
Cannot explain why write blockers are essential
Has no concept of timestomping or artifact manipulation
Thinks deleted files are always recoverable with basic tools
Cannot explain what the Master File Table is or does
